title: Malachi 4
subtitle: The Day of the LORD: Sun of Righteousness and the Return of Elijah

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — "The Sun of Righteousness Will Rise with Healing in Its Rays"

heb.paragraph: The image of the 'sun of righteousness' (shemesh tsedaqah) is unique in the Hebrew Bible and richly layered. The sun is the most powerful and universal source of light and warmth; attaching 'righteousness' to it personifies God's justice and salvation as radiant, life-giving energy. The 'healing in its rays' (marpeʼ bikhnafeyha, literally 'healing in its wings') uses the word khanaf ('wing') which also describes the edges of the sun disk in ancient Near Eastern solar iconography. Unlike pagan solar worship, Malachi does not identify God with the sun but uses solar imagery as a metaphor for the saving effects of divine righteousness breaking into a darkened world.

The promise to send 'the prophet Elijah' (ʼEliyyah hannaviʼ, 4:5) before 'the great and dreadful day of the LORD' is the final prophetic word of the Old Testament in the Christian canonical order. Elijah, who was taken to heaven without dying (2 Kgs 2:11), is the only named individual whose return is prophesied. His mission is reconciliation: 'He will turn the hearts of the parents to their children, and the hearts of the children to their parents' (4:6). The New Testament identifies this Elijah figure with John the Baptist (Matt 11:14; 17:10-13; Luke 1:17), while Jewish tradition associates Elijah with the messianic age and assigns him a place at the Passover table.

refs.note: Jesus identifies John the Baptist as 'the Elijah who was to come,' explicitly connecting Malachi 4:5's prophecy to the Baptist's ministry of preparation.
The angel Gabriel announces that John will go before the Lord 'in the spirit and power of Elijah, to turn the hearts of the parents to their children' — directly citing Malachi 4:6.
Elijah's bodily ascension to heaven in a chariot of fire provides the narrative basis for the expectation of his return: having never died, he is uniquely available for re-entry into history.
After the Transfiguration, Jesus explains that 'Elijah has already come' in the person of John the Baptist, confirming Malachi's prophecy while redefining its scope.

notes.note: The day that 'burns like a furnace' is the final intensification of the refining imagery. In 3:2-3, the fire purified the Levites; here it consumes the arrogant entirely. The distinction between purification (for the faithful) and destruction (for the wicked) is absolute: same fire, opposite results.

The 'sun of righteousness' is not a personal title for the Messiah but a metaphor for the saving effect of God's justice. When divine righteousness rises like the sun, it brings 'healing' to those who have suffered under injustice and darkness. The 'healing in its rays' reverses every wound inflicted by the community's corruption.

The faithful will 'trample on the wicked' who become 'ashes under the soles of your feet.' This is complete eschatological reversal: the arrogant who prospered while the faithful suffered (3:14-15) are now underfoot. Justice is not merely declared but visibly enacted.

The command to 'remember the law of my servant Moses' is not a new demand but a concluding summary: everything Malachi has said about sacrifice, priesthood, marriage, tithes, and justice is rooted in the Mosaic covenant. The prophet calls the community back to its constitutional document.

The promise of Elijah's return is the Old Testament's final word, creating an expectation that will not be fulfilled for over four centuries. The mission of reconciliation — turning hearts of parents to children and children to parents — addresses the generational rupture that underlies the entire prophetic critique: the ancestors sinned, the current generation repeats the pattern, and the children inherit the consequences. Elijah's work is to break this cycle.

Calvin read the final chapter as the summation of all prophetic hope: judgment for the wicked, salvation for the faithful, and the assurance that God's redemptive purposes will not fail. The 'sun of righteousness' is the gospel itself, rising to dispel the darkness of sin and bring healing to wounded souls. The Elijah prophecy points to John the Baptist and, beyond him, to the ongoing ministry of prophetic preparation for Christ's kingdom.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The 'wings' (kenafayim) of the sun of righteousness reflects the winged solar disk iconography common throughout the ancient Near East (Egyptian, Persian, Mesopotamian). The NET notes observe that Malachi appropriates pagan imagery while stripping it of idolatrous content: the healing sun is not a deity but a metaphor for YHWH's righteous intervention.

The reference to 'Horeb' rather than 'Sinai' for the mountain of law-giving follows the Deuteronomic tradition (Deut 1:6; 4:10; 5:2), which consistently uses Horeb, while the Priestly and Elohist sources prefer Sinai. This may indicate Deuteronomic influence on Malachi.

The identification of the returning Elijah as 'the prophet' (hannaviʼ) gives him a specific role: he comes not as a wonder-worker (his primary role in Kings) but as a prophetic voice of reconciliation. The NET notes observe that the mission of 'turning hearts' is pastoral rather than political or military.

verhoef.source: Pieter A. Verhoef, Haggai and Malachi, NICOT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Verhoef reads the furnace-and-sun contrast as Malachi's eschatological climax: the same 'day' that destroys the wicked also heals the righteous. The metaphorical duality — fire as destruction for some, sunlight as healing for others — resolves the theodicy question that has driven the book: the distinction between righteous and wicked will be made permanently visible on the day of the LORD.

Verhoef identifies the closing commands as a 'canonical conclusion' that bridges the prophetic corpus to both the Torah (remember Moses) and the eschatological future (expect Elijah). The twofold imperative creates what Verhoef calls a 'hermeneutical framework': the community is to live between Torah memory and prophetic hope, obedient in the present while expectant for the future.

hill.source: Andrew E. Hill, Malachi, Anchor Bible (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Hill identifies the furnace-and-sun duality as the final answer to the theodicy question: the arrogant will be reduced to stubble and ashes, while the God-fearers will leap for joy under the healing rays of the sun of righteousness. The eschatological day resolves every complaint, every cynical question, and every demand for justice that has surfaced in the book's disputations.

Hill reads the closing verses as a 'canonical epilogue' designed to position Malachi at the intersection of Torah and prophecy, law and eschatology. The command to remember Moses anchors the community in covenant obligation; the promise of Elijah opens the horizon to eschatological restoration. Hill notes that the final word of the Hebrew Bible in the Christian canon is cherem ('curse/destruction'), creating an ominous tension that only the New Testament's arrival of John the Baptist and Christ can resolve.

hist.context: Malachi 4 (3:19-24 in the Hebrew text) is the conclusion of both Malachi and, in the Christian canonical order, of the entire Old Testament prophetic corpus. The chapter divides into three units: the Day of the LORD as furnace fire (4:1), the sun of righteousness rising for the faithful (4:2-3), and the closing commandments to remember Moses' law and expect Elijah's return (4:4-6). The day that 'burns like a furnace' (4:1) continues the refining imagery of 3:2-3 but intensifies it: the arrogant and evildoers will become 'stubble' consumed completely. For the God-fearers, the same day brings not destruction but healing: the 'sun of righteousness' rises with 'healing in its rays,' and they go out and 'frolic like well-fed calves' (4:2) — an image of exuberant liberation. The book closes with two imperatives: remember Moses' torah (4:4) and expect Elijah's return (4:5-6). These two commands bridge backward (to the law-giving at Sinai/Horeb) and forward (to the eschatological day), encapsulating the entire span of Israel's faith: Torah obedience in the present, prophetic hope for the future.

rows.label: vv.1-6
rows.text: Eschatological finale: furnace fire for the wicked, sun of righteousness for the faithful, remember Moses, expect Elijah

lit.note: Chapter 4 closes both Malachi and the Old Testament prophetic canon with a final eschatological vision and two imperatives: remember the Torah (backward to Sinai) and await the prophet (forward to the Day of the LORD).

themes.note: Chapter 4 reaches maximum Prophecy intensity (the sun of righteousness, the return of Elijah as the bridge to the NT) and strong Judgment (the furnace day destroying the wicked), Sovereignty (the LORD controlling the eschatological day), and Justice (the definitive distinction between righteous and wicked).

ppl.name: Levi
ppl.text: In 3:2-3, the fire purified the Levites…

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Elijah
ppl.text: The chapter divides into three units: the Day of the LORD as furnace fire (4:1), the sun of righteousness rising for the faithful (4:2-3), and the closing commandments to remember Moses' law and expect Elijah's return (4:4-6)…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Day of the LORD: Sun of Righteousness and the Return of Elijah

translations.text: “Remember the law of my servant Moses, the decrees and laws I gave him at Horeb for all Israel.
“Remember the law of my servant Moses, the statutes and rules that I commanded him at Horeb for all Israel.
Then you will trample on the wicked; they will be ashes under the soles of your feet on the day when I act,” says the Lord Almighty.
And you shall tread down the wicked, for they will be ashes under the soles of your feet, on the day when I act, says the LORD of hosts.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Day of the LORD within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Jesus identifies John the Baptist as 'the Elijah who was to come,' explicitly connecting Malachi 4:5's prophecy to the Baptist's ministry of preparation.
The angel Gabriel announces that John will go before the Lord 'in the spirit and power of Elijah, to turn the hearts of the parents to their children' — directly citing Malachi 4:6.
Elijah's bodily ascension to heaven in a chariot of fire provides the narrative basis for the expectation of his return: having never died, he is uniquely available for re-entry into history.
After the Transfiguration, Jesus explains that 'Elijah has already come' in the person of John the Baptist, confirming Malachi's prophecy while redefining its scope.

coaching.tip: 'The day is coming, burning like an oven' (v. 1). The arrogant become stubble; the righteous see 'the sun of righteousness rise with healing in its wings' (v. 2). Same day, opposite experiences. Fire consumes or heals depending on the receiver. Judgment sorts; it doesn't uniformly destroy.

'I will send you Elijah the prophet before the great and awesome day of the LORD' (v. 5). The Old Testament ends awaiting Elijah. Jesus identifies John the Baptist as Elijah's fulfillment (Matt 11:14). The last word of the Hebrew Bible points forward — an unfinished sentence the NT completes.
